section.title: Boaz Redeems Ruth
section.sectionRationale: The fourth act is the legal resolution at the city gate. Boaz confronts the nearer kinsman, who agrees to redeem the land until he learns it means marrying Ruth and raising up the dead man's name, then withdraws. Boaz publicly redeems all that belonged to Elimelech and takes Ruth as his wife, and the elders bless the union. It is one movement in which redemption is secured before witnesses.
section.thesis: At the gate the willing redeemer pays the price the nearer kinsman will not, publicly acquiring both the inheritance and the widow, so that the name of the dead is preserved and a Moabite outsider is drawn into the covenant line under the blessing of the whole assembly.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Boaz convenes the nearer kinsman and the elders; the nearer redeemer withdraws; the sandal seals the exchange; and Boaz redeems Ruth to public witness and blessing.

units.label: Boaz convenes the gate
units.note: the case laid before the elders

verses.text: Meanwhile, Boaz went to the {{gate|gate}} and sat down there. Soon the {{goel|kinsman-redeemer}} of whom he had spoken came along, and Boaz said, “Come over here, my friend, and sit down.” So he went over and sat down.
Then Boaz took ten of the elders of the city and said, “Sit here,” and they did so.
And he said to the kinsman-redeemer, “Naomi, who has returned from the land of Moab, is selling the piece of land that belonged to our brother Elimelech.

I thought I should inform you that you may buy it back in the presence of those seated here and in the presence of the elders of my people. If you want to {{gaal|redeem}} it, do so. But if you will not redeem it, tell me so I may know, because there is no one but you to redeem it, and I am next after you.” “I will redeem it,” he replied.

Then Boaz said, “On the day you buy the land from Naomi and also from Ruth the Moabitess, you must also acquire the widow of the deceased in order to raise up the name of the deceased on his inheritance.”
The kinsman-redeemer replied, “I cannot redeem it myself, or I would jeopardize my own inheritance. Take my right of redemption, because I cannot redeem it.”

Now in former times in Israel, concerning the redemption or exchange of property, to make any matter legally binding a man would remove his sandal and give it to the other party, and this was a confirmation in Israel.
So the kinsman-redeemer removed his sandal and said to Boaz, “Buy it for yourself.”
At this, Boaz said to the elders and all the people, “You are witnesses today that I am buying from Naomi all that belonged to Elimelech, Chilion, and Mahlon.

Moreover, I have acquired Ruth the Moabitess, Mahlon’s widow, as my wife, to raise up the name of the deceased through his inheritance, so that his name will not disappear from among his brothers or from the gate of his home. You are witnesses today.”

“We are witnesses,” said the elders and all the people at the gate. “May the LORD make the woman entering your home like Rachel and Leah, who together built up the house of Israel. May you be prosperous in Ephrathah and famous in Bethlehem.
And may your house become like the house of {{perez|Perez}}, whom Tamar bore to Judah, because of the offspring the LORD will give you by this young woman.”

gate.gloss: The city gate. In an Israelite town the gate was the place of legal business, public witness, and the elders' decisions, functioning as the courtroom and civic center. Boaz settles the redemption there so the transaction is binding, public, and beyond dispute.

goel.gloss: Kinsman-redeemer or guardian-redeemer, the near relative legally responsible to rescue his kin, buy back family land, and raise up a name for the dead. The BSB footnote points to Leviticus 25:25-55. The whole scene turns on who will act as goel: the nearer kinsman refuses, and Boaz gladly takes the role.

gaal.gloss: To redeem, act as kinsman-redeemer, or buy back. The verb behind the noun goel; at the gate the nearer relative first says he will redeem, then withdraws when he learns it includes marrying Ruth and raising up the dead man's name. The same root is used by the prophets of the LORD redeeming His people, Isaiah 41:14.

perez.gloss: Perez, a son of Judah by Tamar, born through another account of levirate duty and unexpected blessing, Genesis 38. The elders bless Boaz's house to be like the house of Perez; the closing genealogy runs Perez to Boaz to David, and Matthew 1 carries that same line on to Jesus the Messiah.

basic.context: Boaz goes to the city gate, the place of legal business, and gathers the nearer kinsman and ten elders as witnesses. He offers the nearer redeemer the chance to buy Elimelech's land, which he accepts, until he learns it also means marrying Ruth and raising up the dead man's heir, at which point he backs out. Boaz then publicly redeems the land and takes Ruth as his wife, and the whole assembly blesses the marriage and prays for many children.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a legal, public scene that shows redemption done rightly and openly. The nearer kinsman is not villainous; he simply will not pay the personal cost, which throws Boaz's willing sacrifice into relief. The removed sandal is an ancient legal custom the narrator explains for later readers. The elders' blessing ties this ordinary marriage to the great line of promise.
basic.originalLanguages: The scene unfolds at the gate, shaar, Israel's courtroom. It turns on the goel, the kinsman-redeemer, and the verb gaal, to redeem: the nearer man will not redeem, but Boaz will. The closing blessing invokes Perez, tying this family to Judah's line and forward to David and Christ.
basic.typology: Boaz redeeming what the nearer kinsman could not is a vivid picture of Christ, who accomplishes the redemption that the law and every other would-be rescuer could not. Boaz pays the full price to gain a bride and preserve a name, foreshadowing Christ who pays everything to win His bride and give us His name.
basic.christ: Boaz is the willing redeemer who counts the cost and pays it in full, unlike the nearer kinsman who spares himself. So Jesus, unlike anyone else who might have saved us, willingly gave up His own inheritance, His very life, to redeem us and make us His own. He publicly, openly secured our redemption, and He brings outsiders like Ruth into the family of God.
basic.god: God is at work through law, custom, and human choices to accomplish redemption. We learn that God brings the outsider into His covenant people, that He preserves the name and future of the seemingly forgotten, and that His purposes advance through public, ordinary, faithful acts, all under His sovereign hand.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 25 gives the levirate duty behind the scene. Genesis 38 tells of Perez, Tamar, and Judah. And Christ as the Redeemer who wins His bride appears in Galatians 4 and Revelation 5.
basic.oneStory: Redemption is secured at the gate as the willing redeemer pays the price the other would not. Boaz gaining Ruth and preserving Elimelech's name points straight to Christ, who redeems His people at the cost of His own life, welcomes outsiders, and secures the line that leads to David and to Jesus.

context.literary: The climactic legal scene that resolves the tension raised at the threshing floor. It is structured as a public negotiation: the setup, the nearer kinsman's acceptance, the twist of the marriage obligation, his withdrawal, the sealing sandal, and the ringing declaration and blessing. The naming of Rachel, Leah, Perez, and Tamar lifts a village marriage into the sweep of Israel's founding.
context.historical: The city gate was where elders sat to witness contracts and settle disputes; ten elders made the matter fully official. Levirate custom, Deuteronomy 25:5-10, required a relative to marry a childless widow and raise up an heir for the dead, which explains why the land and the widow came as a package. Removing a sandal was a recognized way to transfer a right or seal a deal.
context.canonical: The redeemer theme reaches its resolution here and becomes a lasting image of Christ's redemption. The blessing's roll call, Rachel, Leah, Tamar, and Perez, deliberately ties Ruth and Boaz to the patriarchal promises and to Judah's line, setting up the genealogy that ends in David and, in the New Testament, in Jesus, Matthew 1:3-6.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show redemption lawfully and publicly secured by a willing redeemer, to contrast Boaz's costly love with the nearer kinsman's self-protection, and to tie this marriage, through the elders' blessing, to the covenant line that leads to David and the Messiah.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes ancient legal customs, the sandal and the levirate obligation, which are not binding on believers today. What endures is the picture of a willing redeemer who pays the full price, the welcome of the outsider into God's people, and God's faithfulness to preserve His covenant line.

debates.question: Why did the nearer kinsman back out once Ruth was included?

views.label: Financial self-protection
views.case: He feared that raising up an heir for the dead would divide or absorb his own estate, since the child would inherit Elimelech's land; unwilling to jeopardize his own inheritance, he declined, sparing himself the cost.

views.label: Unwillingness to take on the whole burden
views.case: Redeeming land was gain, but marrying a Moabite widow and committing to raise a family for another man's name was a heavier, less advantageous duty; he wanted the asset without the obligation.

debates.bearing: Either way his refusal magnifies Boaz's willing, costly love, and points to Christ, who did not spare Himself but paid everything to redeem His people.

debates.question: What is the significance of the blessing invoking Perez, Tamar, Rachel, and Leah?

views.label: Linking Ruth to the covenant line
views.case: By naming the mothers who built the house of Israel and the birth of Perez to Judah, the elders place this marriage within the great promise to Judah, Genesis 49:10, and anticipate the royal line that will come from it.

views.label: Honoring God's pattern of unexpected grace
views.case: Tamar and Ruth were both outsiders or irregular figures through whom God nonetheless advanced His purposes; the blessing celebrates a God who works redemption through unlikely people.

debates.bearing: Both readings point forward: this union stands in the line of promise that runs to David and to Christ, in whose genealogy Tamar, Ruth, and Perez all appear.

study.originalLanguages: The action happens at the shaar, the city gate, Israel's courtroom and civic square, where elders witnessed binding agreements. The whole scene turns on the goel, the kinsman-redeemer, and the verb gaal, to redeem: the nearer relative says, I will redeem, until the cost of marrying Ruth and raising up the dead man's name leads him to withdraw, I cannot redeem it. Boaz then redeems it all and acquires Ruth openly, calling the assembly to witness. The elders' blessing invokes Perez, whom Tamar bore to Judah, tying this marriage to Judah's line and pointing ahead to the genealogy that closes the book and reaches David, and through David, Christ.

typology.type: Boaz redeeming what the nearer kinsman could not
typology.fulfillment: Christ accomplishing the redemption that the law and every other rescuer could not, Romans 8:3 and Galatians 4:4-5.

typology.type: The willing redeemer paying the full price to gain a bride
typology.fulfillment: Christ paying everything, His own life, to win and cleanse His bride, the church, Ephesians 5:25 and Revelation 5:9.

typology.type: The Moabite outsider brought into the covenant line before witnesses
typology.fulfillment: The nations openly welcomed into God's people through Christ, Galatians 3:28-29.

study.christ: Boaz is the willing redeemer who counts the cost and pays it in full, in sharp contrast to the nearer kinsman who will not risk his own inheritance. So Jesus, our true Kinsman-Redeemer, did not spare Himself; He gave up His inheritance and His life to redeem us, Galatians 4:4-5. He accomplished openly and publicly what no one else could, buying His people at the price of His blood, Revelation 5:9. And just as Boaz took a Moabite widow as his bride and preserved a forgotten name, Christ welcomes outsiders, gives us His own name, and secures our place in the family of God forever.
study.god: God's sovereign providence works through law, custom, and free human decisions to bring redemption to completion. We learn that the LORD delights to bring the outsider into His covenant people, that He preserves the name and future of the seemingly forgotten, and that He advances His purposes not by bypassing ordinary life but through public, faithful, everyday acts at a city gate. The whole scene, resolved so satisfyingly, testifies to a God who is quietly and faithfully steering history toward the King who will come.

crossReferences.note: The levirate law requiring a relative to marry the childless widow and raise up an heir, including the removed sandal, behind this scene.
The birth of Perez to Tamar and Judah, invoked in the elders' blessing over Boaz's house.
God sent His Son to redeem those under the law, so that we might receive adoption, the redemption Boaz foreshadows.
The Lamb who was slain purchased people for God from every tribe and nation, the ultimate redemption.

study.oneStory: At the gate, redemption is secured as the willing redeemer pays the price the nearer kinsman will not. Boaz gaining Ruth as his bride and preserving Elimelech's name points straight to Christ, who redeems His people at the cost of His own life, welcomes outsiders into the family of God, and secures the covenant line that runs to David and, at last, to Jesus. The elders' blessing over Bethlehem quietly prepares the way for the King who will be born there.

sermon.title: “So Boaz Took Ruth”
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Ruth, this message expounds Ruth 4:1-13, the transaction at the gate where Boaz redeems the inheritance and takes Ruth as his wife, valuing righteousness and public integrity. A faithful, expository handling of this movement's redemption scene.

quiz.q: Where did Boaz go to settle the matter of redemption?

options.text: To the city gate, the place of legal business and public witness before the elders.
options.note: Ruth 4:1-2 shows Boaz convening the nearer kinsman and ten elders at the gate.

options.text: To the temple in Jerusalem.
options.note: There was no temple yet, and legal matters were settled at the town gate, not a temple.

options.text: To Naomi's house in private.
options.note: Boaz deliberately made it public at the gate, not private.

options.text: To the threshing floor again.
options.note: The threshing floor was the previous scene; the redemption was settled at the gate.

quiz.q: Why did the nearer kinsman refuse to redeem in the end?

options.text: Redeeming meant marrying Ruth and raising an heir for the dead, which he feared would jeopardize his own inheritance.
options.note: Ruth 4:5-6 shows he withdrew rather than risk his estate.

options.text: He did not have enough money to buy the land.
options.note: He first agreed to buy the land; it was the added obligation of marriage and heir that made him withdraw.

options.text: He disliked Boaz personally.
options.note: The issue was cost to his inheritance, not personal dislike.

options.text: The elders forbade him from redeeming.
options.note: The elders were witnesses; the kinsman chose to withdraw himself.

quiz.q: How was the transfer of the right of redemption sealed?

options.text: The nearer kinsman removed his sandal and gave it to Boaz, an ancient sign of confirmation.
options.note: Ruth 4:7-8 explains the sandal custom that made the matter legally binding.

options.text: They signed a written contract.
options.note: The binding sign here was the removed sandal, a recognized custom, not a signature.

options.text: They exchanged money in secret.
options.note: The exchange was public and sealed by the sandal, not a secret payment.

options.text: They swore an oath at the altar.
options.note: The custom was the sandal at the gate, not an altar oath.

quiz.q: What did Boaz declare before all the witnesses?

options.text: That he had bought all of Elimelech's holdings and acquired Ruth as his wife to preserve the dead man's name.
options.note: Ruth 4:9-10 records his public declaration of redemption and marriage.

options.text: That he was giving the land back to the nearer kinsman.
options.note: The opposite: Boaz took on the redemption the kinsman refused.

options.text: That Ruth must return to Moab.
options.note: He took Ruth as his wife into the covenant people, not back to Moab.

options.text: That Naomi owed him a debt.
options.note: Boaz redeemed for Naomi and Ruth's sake; there was no debt claimed against her.

quiz.q: How does Boaz's redemption picture the work of Christ?

options.text: The willing redeemer pays the full price the other would not, winning a bride and preserving a name, as Christ redeems us at the cost of His life.
options.note: Boaz foreshadows Christ, who did not spare Himself to redeem His people, Galatians 4:4-5.

options.text: It shows redemption is cheap and costs the redeemer nothing.
options.note: The whole point is the cost the willing redeemer pays; redemption is never cheap.

options.text: It teaches that outsiders can never be included.
options.note: Ruth the Moabitess is brought in, picturing the welcome of the nations.

options.text: It proves the nearer kinsman was the true redeemer.
options.note: The nearer kinsman withdrew; Boaz is the redeemer who foreshadows Christ.

questions: The nearer kinsman would not pay the personal cost of redeeming; Boaz gladly did. Where is God calling you to costly love rather than self-protection?
Boaz settled the redemption openly, before witnesses. How does the public, finished nature of Christ's redemption give you assurance today?
Ruth the Moabite outsider was drawn into the covenant family and given a new name and future. How does this shape the way you view your own place in God's family?
The elders blessed the marriage and tied it to God's larger promises. How can you learn to see God's bigger purposes at work in ordinary events like a wedding or a workplace decision?
Christ, our Redeemer, did not spare Himself to win us. How does His willing sacrifice move your worship and your obedience this week?

translationNotes.note: Kinsman-redeemer renders goel throughout the chapter, a legal term for the relative who redeems his kin; the BSB footnote points to Leviticus 25:25-55.
The removed sandal was an old Israelite custom for confirming a transfer of right or property; the narrator explains it because the practice had faded by the time of writing.
Rachel and Leah built up the house of Israel, and Ephrathah is the region of Bethlehem; the blessing places Ruth among the mothers of the covenant people.
The house of Perez, whom Tamar bore to Judah, Genesis 38, ties this marriage to Judah's line and forward to the genealogy of David and Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Ruth 4:1-12. Verse 4 follows the BSB reading you will not redeem it, noted in the source as supported by many manuscripts and versions.
